recording_notes: Post-training V10 run on the original training station. Deliberate, controlled run with atraumatic drain handling. Some video-angle limitations on exact mark deviation and wrap count, but the knot structure and closure outcome are clearly assessable.

frame_analyses.description: The first instrument tip becomes visible from the right and the trainee begins bringing the needle-suture complex into the field.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Task timing starts here; entry is controlled but setup is not immediate.

frame_analyses.description: The needle is being reoriented for the initial bite with both instruments working near the Penrose.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Several small regrasps are used to achieve a workable needle angle before the pass.

frame_analyses.description: The needle is driven through the Penrose near the marked targets while the opposite instrument stabilizes and elevates the drain.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Control is steady and tissue handling is gentle, though the bite sequence is deliberate rather than efficient.

frame_analyses.description: The suture has been drawn through and the trainee transitions from placement to intracorporeal knot tying.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: This is the clean handoff point from needle passage to loop formation.

frame_analyses.description: The first throw is being advanced toward the slit and seated over the Penrose.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Loop management is wide and somewhat slow, but the throw appears to be laid down centrally.

frame_analyses.description: An alternating second throw is formed and tightened with the knot stack flattening over the slit.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: The loop direction appears reversed from the first throw, suggesting an appropriate hand switch.

frame_analyses.description: The final throw is tightened and the knot becomes compact at the center of the Penrose.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: The trainee maintains control without obvious slippage, though there are extra corrective movements before final seating.

frame_analyses.description: Scissors are in the field and the suture tails are being isolated for intracorporeal cutting.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Tail control is cautious; this phase adds time because the ends are repeatedly repositioned before cutting.

frame_analyses.description: Both suture ends have been cut and the final knot remains seated on the Penrose without avulsion.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: End state appears stable with the slit approximated and the knot still centered.

knot_assessments.notes: First throw appears to be a double-wrapped surgeon's knot with the right instrument leading, although the exact wrap count is partially obscured during loop transfer.
Second throw appears to reverse loop direction and is best interpreted as a single alternating throw led by the left instrument.
Final throw appears to return to a right-sided lead and seats without obvious loosening or unraveling.

suture_placement.placement_notes: Bite appears close to both target marks; millimetric deviation is video-angle limited.

drain_assessment.assessment_note: No visible gap at the knot site, drain not avulsed, knot stack remains seated through the cutting phase.

technique_summary: This was a deliberate, controlled performance with suture bites that appeared close to the marks and a final knot that remained seated after cutting. The main weakness was efficiency: there were repeated regrasps, large loop management during the throws, and a prolonged cutting phase. Tissue handling stayed gentle, the drain was not avulsed, and slit approximation appeared complete at the end.
improvement_suggestions: Load the needle to a stable working angle earlier; the initial bite setup involved several extra regrasps that added time before the first pass.
Keep a shorter working end and reduce oversized loops during knot tying so each throw can be transferred and seated with fewer corrective movements.
After each throw, apply immediate opposing tension to seat the knot flat over the slit rather than spending extra time recentering the throw before tightening.
strengths: Needle placement appeared close to the marked targets despite a constrained workspace.
The Penrose was handled atraumatically with no avulsion or obvious tearing during the task.
The final knot appeared secure and centered, and both suture ends were successfully cut intracorporeally.
comparison_notes: Major drain-gap divergence vs Claude postV10. Claude reported gap_visible=true / slit_closure=partial and applied 12.5 total penalties (4.5 placement + 8.0 quality/efficiency/gap); GPT-4o reports gap_visible=false / slit_closure=complete and applies rubric-strict 2.5 placement-only. Score delta 13.3 FLS (385.8 vs 372.5). This is the first drain-gap divergence in the series outside the V11-V20 window and the eighth out-of-rubric penalty case for Claude (V20, V22, V26, V28, V29, V30, V31, postV10). Time agreement is tight (delta 3.3s) with the same INVERSE cut-phase boundary pattern as V30/V31: GPT-4o cut 36.2s, Claude cut 50s; throws 115s vs 80s — Claude pushes work into cut while GPT-4o keeps it in throws. Hand-lead alternation pattern agrees (R-L-R on both sides).
